text:
GYNECOLOGIC ONCOLOGY CLINIC - SURVEILLANCE NOTE

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: Gynecologic Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Established Patient, Surveillance

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Routine follow-up visit. Patient with history of stage IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, currently in complete remission on nira maintenance therapy.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Delacroix is a 54-year-old postmenopausal woman with a well-established history of FIGO stage IIIC (pT3c pN1 M0) high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, initially diagnosed approximately 27 months ago, harboring a germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (5382insC, c.5266dupC). She presents today for her routine surveillance visit.

To briefly summarize her oncologic history: she was initially diagnosed with bilateral ovarian masses with extensive peritoneal carcinomatosis and ascites. She underwent primary debulking surgery with exploratory laparotomy, TAH/BSO, bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, infracolic omentectomy, appendectomy, and diaphragmatic peritoneal stripping, achieving a complete gross resection (R0). She then completed 6 cycles of Paraplatin AUC 6 / PTX 175 mg/m2 (PTX ultimately dose-reduced to 135 mg/m2 in cycles 5 and 6 due to cumulative Grade 3 peripheral neuropathy with left foot drop). She achieved complete biochemical and radiographic remission -- CA-125 normalized to 18 U/mL at the end of chemotherapy, and PET-CT at that time showed no FDG-avid disease. She was subsequently started on nira (Zejula) maintenance at an individualized starting dose of 200 mg daily given her weight of 72 kg. During week 3 of nira, she developed Grade 3 thrombocytopenia (platelet nadir 42,000/uL), which prompted a hold followed by dose reduction to 150 mg daily. At 150 mg, she has tolerated nira well with stable Grade 1 thrombocytopenia and Grade 1 anemia. She is now approximately 18 months into nira maintenance.

At today's visit, she reports doing very well overall. She denies any new abdominal pain, bloating, early satiety, or change in bowel habits. No nausea or vomiting. No dyspnea or chest pain. No new lymphadenopathy. She does endorse very mild, intermittent tingling in her toes bilaterally with prolonged standing, which she attributes to residual Taxol-related neuropathy -- she states this is barely noticeable and is not interfering with any activities. She denies worsening of neuropathic symptoms. Foot drop resolved completely several months ago following physical therapy. Energy levels are good. She has returned to full-time work as a high school librarian and is engaging in regular daily walks. She takes nira 150 mg each evening with food and reports good adherence -- no missed doses in the past month.

She notes mild persistent hot flashes, managed adequately with venlafaxine 37.5 mg daily. Vaginal dryness managed with vaginal lubricants. She denies any new breast symptoms. Her most recent breast MRI, performed approximately 2 months ago, was negative.

Regarding psychosocial status: she previously experienced some anxiety related to fear of recurrence and was connected with the oncology social work team and an ovarian cancer survivors' support group. She reports these have been very helpful. She feels emotionally well and states she has come to terms with her diagnosis and finds meaning in being an advocate within the support group for newly diagnosed patients.

Her daughter (age 24) has been confirmed to carry the BRCA1 5382insC variant and is being followed in the high-risk genetics program. The patient notes this has been a source of ongoing concern, but she is grateful the variant was identified and her daughter is under appropriate surveillance.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Constitutional: No fever, chills, or unintentional weight loss. Good appetite. Energy adequate.
Pulmonary: No dyspnea, cough, or hemoptysis.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or lower extremity edema.
Gastrointestinal: No abdominal pain, distension, nausea, vomiting, or change in bowel habits. No rectal bleeding.
Genitourinary: No hematuria or dysuria. No pelvic pressure.
Musculoskeletal: No new joint pain or bone pain.
Neurologic: Residual mild tingling in bilateral toes as noted above, non-progressive. No new neurologic symptoms.
Hematologic: No easy bruising, petechiae, or bleeding. No epistaxis or gingival bleeding.
Skin: Hair regrowth complete. No rash.
Psychiatric: Mild background anxiety, improving with support group participation and social work engagement.

---

MEDICATIONS:
1. nira (Zejula) 150 mg orally once daily (each evening with food)
2. Venlafaxine 37.5 mg orally once daily (vasomotor symptoms)
3. Vaginal lubricant (Replens) as needed
4. Calcium carbonate 600 mg with vitamin D 400 IU orally twice daily (bone health, post-surgical menopause)
5. Multivitamin once daily

ALLERGIES: NKDA

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
General: Well-appearing woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented. Hair regrown to normal length and density.
Vitals: Blood pressure 118/72 mmHg, heart rate 72 bpm, temperature 36.8 degrees C, weight 73.1 kg, BMI 26.8 kg/m2, SpO2 98% on room air.
HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Oropharynx clear, no mucositis.
Neck: No cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary lymphadenopathy palpated.
Chest/Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No dullness to percussion.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs.
Breasts: No dominant masses palpated bilaterally. No axillary adenopathy.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No organomegaly. No palpable masses. Midline laparotomy scar well-healed. No evidence of abdominal wall herniation.
Pelvic (limited external exam): No pelvic masses on bimanual palpation. Vaginal vault without lesions. No pelvic tenderness.
Extremities: No peripheral edema. No lower extremity tenderness.
Neurologic: Cranial nerves grossly intact. Gait normal and steady. No foot drop. Sensation to light touch mildly diminished at tips of toes bilaterally (consistent with Grade 1 residual sensory neuropathy), no significant sensory deficit in hands or proximal lower extremities. Deep tendon reflexes intact.
Skin: No petechiae, purpura, or ecchymoses.

---

LAB RESULTS (obtained today):
CA-125: 11 U/mL (normal, reference <35 U/mL) -- stable compared with 12 U/mL six months ago and 14 U/mL at the 15-month mark. Reassuring trend.
CBC:
  - WBC: 4,800/uL (normal)
  - ANC: 2,100/uL (normal)
  - Hemoglobin: 11.1 g/dL (Grade 1 anemia, stable)
  - Platelets: 119,000/uL (Grade 1 thrombocytopenia, stable; no change from prior values of 108,000-125,000/uL range)
  - MCV: 89 fL (normocytic)
Comprehensive metabolic panel:
  - Creatinine: 0.80 mg/dL (stable, no renal toxicity from prior Paraplatin)
  - BUN: 14 mg/dL
  - AST/ALT: within normal limits
  - Alkaline phosphatase: within normal limits
  - Albumin: 4.1 g/dL
No evidence of dysplastic changes on CBC differential. No blasts. No toxic granulation.

---

IMAGING:
Most recent CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast (obtained approximately 6 months prior, at the 21-month post-diagnosis mark): No evidence of disease recurrence. Stable post-operative pelvic changes. No ascites, no peritoneal nodules, no lymphadenopathy. Surveillance CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast ordered today and scheduled for approximately 6 months from now (next routine imaging interval).

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. HIGH-GRADE SEROUS OVARIAN CARCINOMA, FIGO STAGE IIIC, IN COMPLETE REMISSION:
Ms. Delacroix continues to do exceptionally well, now 27 months out from her initial diagnosis and 18 months into Zejula maintenance therapy. CA-125 is 11 U/mL -- at its lowest yet -- and has been stable and normal for over a year. No clinical, biochemical, or radiographic evidence of disease recurrence. The achievement and durability of complete remission in the context of germline BRCA1, HRD-positive, stage IIIC disease following R0 resection and platinum-based chemotherapy is consistent with the robust benefit seen in the SOLO-1 data for BRCA-mutant ovarian cancer on PARP inhibitor maintenance. We will continue Zejula 150 mg daily without modification.

2. nira MAINTENANCE THERAPY -- HEMATOLOGIC MONITORING:
Zejula 150 mg daily ongoing. Platelet count today at 119,000/uL -- stable Grade 1 thrombocytopenia, which has been the patient's plateau since the dose reduction from 200 mg to 150 mg following the Grade 3 thrombocytopenic event at week 3. No further dose reduction required. Hemoglobin 11.1 g/dL -- stable Grade 1 anemia, no transfusion dependency, no symptoms of anemia reported (no exertional dyspnea, no symptomatic fatigue). ANC normal. CBC differential without dysplastic features -- no concern for treatment-related MDS at this time. Will continue monthly CBC monitoring per routine. Patient and I discussed that the risk of MDS/AML with PARP inhibitor therapy is real but low (approximately 1-2% with long-term use), and periodic CBC monitoring with attention to unexplained cytopenias is the appropriate surveillance strategy. I emphasized that her current cytopenias are drug-related and stable and do not represent dysplasia.

3. RESIDUAL PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY (PTX-RELATED):
Grade 1 residual sensory neuropathy in bilateral toes, non-progressive and now essentially stable for many months. Patient not functionally limited. Foot drop completely resolved. No further intervention required. Patient counseled to report any worsening.

4. BRCA1 GERMLINE VARIANT AND BREAST CANCER SURVEILLANCE:
Patient is BRCA1 5382insC carrier, post-BSO (ovarian/fallopian tube risk addressed surgically). Regarding breast surveillance: most recent breast MRI approximately 2 months ago was negative. Annual mammography and breast MRI alternating every 6 months per NCCN guidelines for BRCA1 carriers discussed. Option of risk-reducing mastectomy revisited -- patient remains on surveillance approach, which is her informed preference, and I respect that decision. She is up to date with breast surveillance. I noted that her bilateral BSO at a natural menopausal age (she was 50 at natural menopause, surgery at 52) likely has less impact on breast cancer risk reduction than it would have at a younger age, but the ovarian/fallopian tube cancer risk has been definitively addressed. Daughter confirmed BRCA1-positive and enrolled in high-risk program -- no further action needed from our end.

5. MENOPAUSAL SYMPTOM MANAGEMENT:
Hot flashes adequately managed with venlafaxine 37.5 mg daily. Vaginal dryness managed with lubricants. Bone health addressed with calcium and vitamin D supplementation. DEXA scan has not been performed recently -- will place order for bone density assessment given surgical menopause and prolonged postmenopausal state, to assess for osteoporosis and guide further management.

6. PSYCHOSOCIAL:
Ongoing mild cancer recurrence anxiety. Patient engaged with oncology social work and ovarian cancer survivor support group -- these interventions appear effective. No formal psychiatric referral needed at this time. Patient functioning well.

FOLLOW-UP:
Return to clinic in 3 months for next scheduled surveillance visit with CA-125 and CBC. CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast in approximately 6 months. Continue Zejula 150 mg daily. DEXA scan ordered. Continue annual breast MRI and mammogram per NCCN BRCA1 surveillance guidelines.

Time spent with patient: approximately 35 minutes, including counseling and coordination of care.

Electronically signed,
Attending Physician, Gynecologic Oncology
